title: The Voice in the Wilderness
subtitle: John the Baptist preaches repentance by the Jordan, and his whole life cries out the word of penance

stillness: The word of the Lord is an inward inspiration of the mind,
which is called a word because it speaks within, in the mind.
Be still, and hear what the Lord God speaks within you.
gospel: In those days came John the Baptist,
preaching in the desert of Judea, and saying:
Do penance, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.
And there went out to him all Jerusalem, and all Judea,
and all the country about the Jordan,
and were baptized by him in the Jordan,
Bring forth therefore fruits worthy of penance.
For now the axe is laid to the root of the trees.
Every tree that does not bring forth good fruit
shall be cut down, and cast into the fire.
behold: John had a garment of camel's hair,
rough hair-cloth, not wool, not soft —
a sign of austerity, not of softer luxury,
a garment that wears down the body rather than warms it,
so that the very dress of his body might speak of the virtue of his soul.
He had a belt of leather about his loins,
of dry and rough hides, where the seat of lust is,
to mortify the flesh and its wantonness.
His food was locusts and wild honey,
the food of the poor in that land.
The earth was his bed, the caves his house,
hair his clothing, skin his belt, water his drink, a wild place his table.
Everything that was in John preached penance:
his name, for it is the grace of God;
his place, for it is the desert;
his dress, for it is hair-cloth;
his food, for it is locusts;
his word, for it is the word of penance.
consider: The baptism of John did not cleanse without penance.
His washing could not loose sins;
it was only a kind of protestation of penance to be done,
by which sins are forgiven.
It was the baptism of penance — there pardon, here, in Christ, victory.
Now the axe is laid to the root of the trees:
the passing of this fleeting life is set at the very root of every man,
for from the first moment of his birth a man begins to fall toward his fall.
To live is nothing else than to pass out of life and to tend toward death.
Night, like a woodcutter, takes one chip from the tree, and the day another;
however many days and nights a man has lived, so much less has he to live,
and at the last the tree must fall.
And wherever the tree shall fall, there it shall remain;
where God finds you, there He will judge you —
there, I say, unchangeably and beyond recall.
Would you know to which side the tree will fall? Look to its branches.
Our branches are our desires:
if they are spiritual, we are stretched toward the south;
if they are carnal, toward the north.
It is not enough to turn away from evil; you must also do good.
God loves nothing barren — not in His garden, not in His vineyard, not in His field.
And so, of John's mercy: he who has two coats, let him give to him that has none;
and he who has food, let him do likewise.
Mercy is the fullness of the virtues.
God is mighty to raise up sons of Abraham from these stones —
from the hard and from sinners, to make the devout.
Would that He might deign to show this in me.
prayer: Most blessed John the Baptist, forerunner of Christ and most holy virgin,
who preached penance to sinners by your word
and showed it no less by your example,
leading a life austere in food and clothing,
and withdrawing yourself from the enticements of the world:
I beseech you, obtain for me by your holy prayers
that the Lord may grant me fitting abstinence
of food and drink, of thought, of speech, and of deed;
that He may guard me from all defilement of mind and body,
and grant me, as long as I dwell in this temporal life,
to be set apart from vices, and to do battle for Him,
and so to bring forth fruits worthy of penance,
that I may obtain pardon of all my sins
and come to life everlasting. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Nox enim quasi unus carpentator de dicta arbore tollit unum scopulum, et dies similiter alium; quot enim diebus et noctibus vixit homo, tot minus habet vivere, et ad ultimum necesse habet arbor cadere.
latinJewel.english: For night, like a woodcutter, takes one chip from the tree, and the day likewise takes another; for however many days and nights a man has lived, by so much less has he left to live, and at the last the tree must fall.
